summary: Gruber reviews Apple's HomePod, finding it delivers exactly what Apple promises: excellent audio quality in a compact speaker controlled primarily through Siri, priced at $349. He praises the sound quality as clearly superior to competitors like Google Home Max, Sonos One, and Amazon Echo, and highlights impressive engineering feats like voice recognition during loud music playback. However, he catalogs significant limitations including no third-party Siri integration (notably Spotify), no hardware audio input, single iCloud account support, and missing features like multi-room audio that depend on the delayed AirPlay 2. He frames the purchase decision as primarily about ecosystem investment rather than a simple spec comparison.
key_points: HomePod's audio quality genuinely justifies its $349 price and sounds notably better than Google Home Max ($399) and Sonos One ($199) in side-by-side comparison
Apple designed HomePod as a speaker first and added Siri as the best interface, whereas Amazon built Echo hardware to get Alexa into homes — fundamentally different product philosophies
The lack of third-party Siri integration, multi-user voice recognition, and AirPlay 2 features at launch represents significant gaps that competitors already address
HomePod's ability to hear voice commands at normal speaking volume over loud music is an impressive engineering achievement that surpasses human hearing
The purchase decision should come down to ecosystem investment: Apple ecosystem users get a compelling product, while Google or Spotify users should look elsewhere
money_quotes: HomePod does exactly what Apple says it does, doesn't do anything more than what Apple says it does, and costs $349.
Not supporting Spotify might sell some number of new $10/month Apple Music subscriptions, but supporting Spotify properly would sell some number of $349 HomePods that wouldn't otherwise be purchased.
HomePod is very much a 'skating to where the puck is going to be' product, and Apple has believed for years that when it comes to personal audio, the puck is heading toward a wireless world.
It's the difference between 'Let's make a speaker to get Alexa into the home' and 'Let's use Siri because voice would be the best interface for a home speaker'.
I would go so far as to say that HomePod can understand commands spoken at normal volume while music is playing better than human ears do.
A family device ought to work well for everyone in the family.
The future is coming, and it will be listening to us. And watching us. I trust Apple more than I trust Google or Amazon.
Playing audio via AirPlay is to native 'Hey Siri, play …' support on HomePod as web apps are to native apps on iOS or Mac: better than nothing but clunky compared to the real deal.
key_insight: HomePod is a genuinely great speaker hobbled at launch by missing software features, reflecting Apple's hardware-first philosophy that prioritizes audio quality over the voice assistant capabilities that define its competitors.
